data.title: Albemarle shares clock nine-session winning streak
data.description:
Albemarle Corporation (ALB) shares marked nine straight sessions of gains, as the stock was up 2.8% at $124.76 on Wednesday.

The energy storage solutions company added 25% in the preceding eight sessions. The stock has surged 45.5% so far this year, compared to a 12.5% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index (SP500).

ALB is up 37% over the past one month.

"ALB's asset sales and reduced CAPEX enhance financial flexibility, but upcoming debt maturities and lithium price volatility present risks, while the cash available offers some safety net," pointed out a recent Seeking Alpha analysis. 

Albemarle reported a smaller-than-expected loss for the third quarter, as the lithium producer's tight leash on costs and resilient sales volumes softened the squeeze from persistently low prices for the metal.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, ALB has a Hold rating with a score of 3.3 out of 5. The company received B+ in the prospect of momentum, while it got D- growth factor.

Turning to the Wall Street community, seven analysts gave ALB a Buy and above rating. 17 analysts have given the stock a Hold recommendation, while two recommended Strong Sell.

 

Seeking Alpha analysts are positive and see the stock as a Buy.

data.title: Golub Capital BDC signals review of dividend policy while navigating credit cycle headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO David B. Golub described the quarter as “solid” and the end to fiscal year 2025 as “strong,” highlighting “solid credit results across our portfolio.” He acknowledged, “the private credit direct lending market faces some headwinds and GBDC is not immune from those headwinds.”
  • Golub reported for the quarter, “adjusted NII per share was $0.39, and that translates to an adjusted NII ROE of 10.4%.” For the year, “GBDC paid $1.65 per share of cumulative distributions, representing 10.9% of end-of-year net asset value per share.” He noted the net asset value per share at year-end was $14.97, “$0.34 above GBDC’s net asset value per share at its IPO in 2010.”
  • Golub emphasized, “the direct lending market is facing some headwinds... spreads have narrowed... base rates have started to come down... we’re in a credit cycle.” He said, “I anticipate it’s going to continue for some time.”
  • Announcing a $0.39 per share distribution for the first quarter of fiscal 2026, Golub stated, “the Board also determined that it would be prudent to revisit GBDC’s dividend policy early next year when we hope to have more information on the forward outlook for rates and asset spreads and financing costs.”
  • CFO Christopher Ericson stated, “adjusted NII per share of $0.39 was in line with $0.39 per share base distribution paid out during the quarter and adjusted net realized and unrealized losses were $0.03 per share.” He noted, “GBDC’s weighted average net investment spread... declined modestly quarter-over-quarter to 4.8%.”
  • Tim Topicz highlighted, “approximately 90% of GBDC’s investment portfolio at fair value remains in our highest performing internal rating categories,” and “investments on nonaccrual status decreased to a very low level, 0.3%... well below the BDC peer industry average.”

Outlook

  • Golub stated, “we expect elevated credit stress to persist, and we expect this to continue to impact different BDCs in different ways.” He added, “we expect the gap between winners and whiners to widen, and the winners will be those with proven competitive advantages and a long track record of low credit losses across cycles.”
  • The Board plans to “revisit GBDC’s dividend policy early next year,” seeking “to have more information on the forward outlook for rates and asset spreads and financing costs.”

Financial Results

  • Adjusted NII per share was $0.39 and adjusted net income per share was $0.36.
  • GBDC ended the fiscal year with net asset value per share of $14.97.
  • Portfolio decreased modestly quarter-over-quarter to just under $8.8 billion at fair value, as repayments and exits outpaced new investment commitments.
  • Net debt to equity ended at 1.23x, within the targeted range.
  • Investments on nonaccrual status decreased to 0.3% of the total investment portfolio at fair value.
  • GBDC opportunistically repurchased 368,000 shares in the quarter and an additional 2.5 million shares after quarter end.

Q&A

  • Jordan Wathen, Wells Fargo Securities: Asked about changes in the availability or quality of equity co-investment opportunities. CEO Golub responded, “We have not seen any meaningful change in either our approach or the availability of equity co-invests as we’ve historically done them.”
  • Robert Dodd, Raymond James: Inquired about inflation and sector stress. Golub expressed optimism regarding capital spending benefits from recent legislation but raised concerns about the “subprime consumer,” highlighting “increased delinquencies” in credit card, mortgage, and auto loans. He described the macro picture as “confusing.”
  • Dodd also asked about spread compression risk. Golub explained, “spreads are an indicator of confidence. And right now, investors are talking with their feet that they would rather be invested in debt investments... than in other investments.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on sector stress, inflation, and spread risk, reflecting a slightly cautious tone, particularly with repeated focus on headwinds and the macro environment.
  • Management maintained a measured but confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing resilience and experience. In the Q&A, Golub’s responses were detailed and acknowledged challenges, using phrases such as “I have some optimism” and “I have some concerns,” showing a balanced stance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remains steady, still referencing market uncertainty but reiterating experience and a disciplined strategy. Analyst tone was slightly more probing this quarter, especially on sector risk and portfolio resilience.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Dividend policy now flagged for early review next year, while last quarter the focus was on stability.
  • Net asset value per share declined from $15.00 to $14.97.
  • Investment portfolio decreased to just under $8.8 billion from just under $9 billion.
  • Nonaccruals improved from 0.6% to 0.3% of total investments at fair value.
  • Management’s language on headwinds is more pronounced this quarter, with repeated references to “credit cycle” and “credit stress.”
  • Last quarter, the outlook for M&A activity was more optimistic; this quarter, outlook is tempered by ongoing credit stress.
  • Analyst questions have shifted from leverage and spreads to deeper concerns about inflation, sector risk, and consumer credit health.

Risks and Concerns

  • Key risks cited include “narrowed spreads,” “declining base rates,” and “elevated credit stress” in both public and private credit markets.
  • Golub warned, “we’re in a credit cycle... I anticipate it’s going to continue for some time.”
  • Management is monitoring macroeconomic uncertainty, especially regarding the subprime consumer and potential for further defaults.
  • Credit market volatility and performance dispersion among direct lending managers remain concerns.
  • The Board’s decision to revisit the dividend policy reflects uncertainty about future rates, spreads, and financing costs.

Final Takeaway

Golub Capital BDC concluded fiscal 2025 with resilient earnings, a stable dividend, and a diversified portfolio, but management signaled caution amid credit cycle headwinds and sector-wide spread compression. With a strong liquidity position and disciplined underwriting, the company is preparing to revisit its dividend strategy early next year as it navigates a challenging and uncertain credit environment, focusing on maintaining low credit losses and prioritizing returns to investors.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Carlyle Credit Income Fund signals continued portfolio rotation and targets dividend coverage amid shifting CLO market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Carlyle Credit Income Fund (CCIF) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Nishil Mehta, MD & Portfolio Manager, highlighted that the fund maintained its monthly dividend at $0.105 per share or 24.1% annualized, declared through February 2026. Mehta reported, "The monthly dividend is supported by $0.51 of recurring cash flows for the quarter, providing 162% of dividend coverage." The fund made new CLO investments totaling $34.9 million with a weighted average GAAP yield of 13.7%, while rotating out of 10 CLO investments for $36.5 million. Mehta stated, "We completed 7 refinancings and resets in Q4 2025, reducing the cost of liabilities, extending the reinvestment period and bolstering equity cash flows across these CLOs."
  • Mehta noted, "Tighter loan spreads continue to weigh on portfolio yields and valuations during the quarter," and added that the average cash yield on CLO investments was 21.8% for the quarter, with the weighted average spread on the underlying loan portfolio at 3.12%.
  • Lauren Basmadjian, CEO & Trustee, shared that "CLO issuance totaled $52 billion for the quarter, up from $49 billion in the prior quarter, reflecting a meaningful pickup in activity." She reported that "CLO liability spreads tightened across the capital stack with AAAs moving approximately 10 basis points tighter and BBs compressing 40 basis points during the quarter."
  • Nelson Joseph, Principal, stated, "Total investment income for the fourth quarter was $7.7 million or $0.37 per share. Total expenses for the quarter was $4.6 million. Total net investment income for the fourth quarter was $3.2 million or $0.15 per share." Joseph added, "Adjusted net investment income for the fourth quarter was $3.6 million or $0.17 per share."

Outlook

  • Mehta projected that "refinancing and reset activity to continue, taking advantage of historically tight CLO liability spreads" and expressed an expectation for increased loan activity in 2026, "supported by declining base rates, normalization of tariff and regulatory policy and continued economic growth."
  • Basmadjian described the market as "a market with expanded reinvestment capacity," and stated, "We do not forecast a meaningful increase from today's default rate of 3.5%, which is below the cycle's peak of 4.5% reached in the fourth quarter of 2024."
  • The fund expects portfolio rotation and refinancing to remain a key strategic lever, while expressing confidence in the defensive positioning of the current portfolio.

Financial Results

  • Joseph reported, "The cash-on-cash yield of 21.8% on CLO investment quarterly payments resulted in $0.51 per share of recurring cash flow."
  • "Total net investment income for the fourth quarter was $3.2 million or $0.15 per share," and "adjusted net investment income for the fourth quarter was $3.6 million or $0.17 per share."
  • "Core net investment income for the fourth quarter was $0.32 per share, providing dividend coverage of 102% of our monthly dividend of $0.105 per share."
  • "Net asset value for September 30 was $6.13 per share."
  • The fund entered a $30 million credit facility and issued 7.375% Series D term preferred shares generating net proceeds of approximately $29.4 million and completed a private placement of 7.25% Series E convertible preferred shares for net proceeds of approximately $16.3 million. Proceeds were used to redeem all $52 million of 8.75% Series A term preferred shares, reducing the cost of capital by approximately 1.42%.

Q&A

  • Gaurav Mehta, Alliance Global Partners, Research Division: Asked about investment opportunities between primary and secondary markets. Mehta responded, "We continue to see opportunities across both the secondary and primary markets... We're seeing unique and interesting opportunities in both today."
  • Mehta was unable to provide a breakdown between primary and secondary investments for the quarter, indicating a slight focus on secondary but active participation in both.
  • Gaurav Mehta inquired about the potential for portfolio recycling. Mehta replied, "We continue to look at the portfolio, and we continue to do kind of rotation trades, even post quarter end. So I expect that to continue maybe to a lesser extent to what we've done over the past 6 months though."
  • Erik Zwick, Lucid Capital Markets, LLC, Research Division: Asked about the opportunity for resets and refis. Mehta stated, "It's probably at least another 25% of the portfolio today that could be refinancing or reset candidates."
  • Zwick followed up on recurring cash flow and its relationship to dividend and expenses. Mehta responded, "The number of deals making distributions should increase if the pace of refis and resets slows down."
  • Zwick asked about share buybacks. Mehta said, "It's something that we are constantly discussing internally within senior management and our Board as well... If the fund continues to trade at these considerable discounts, it's something that we will consider in the future."
  • Timothy D'Agostino, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: Asked about the timing for selling the legacy real estate asset. Mehta replied, "It's a piece of land that's open for development outside of Austin... we continue to focus on, on the near term and just able to recycle that into kind of our existing focus on CLO equity."
  • D'Agostino also asked about growth levers for the fund. Mehta emphasized, "The #1 focus right now is obviously making sure -- or trying to get the stock to trade above NAV... What we can control is really how the fund performs."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on portfolio rotation, recurring cash flow, and growth levers, indicating a neutral to slightly cautious tone as they sought explanations for dividend coverage and future fund growth.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing portfolio defensiveness and confidence in dividend coverage. During Q&A, Mehta acknowledged uncertainties but expressed ongoing strategic intent, using phrases such as "we continue to focus on" and "I think it's fair to say."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the overall tone was steady, with both analysts and management focused on addressing persistent market challenges and ongoing portfolio optimization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The fund maintained its monthly dividend at $0.105 per share, but annualized yield rose to 24.1% from 22.1% in the previous quarter.
  • Recurring cash flows supporting the dividend decreased to $0.51 per share from $0.55.
  • Total investment income declined from $8.6 million to $7.7 million, and net investment income fell from $4 million to $3.2 million.
  • The fund increased the pace of refinancings and resets to 7 transactions in Q4 versus 2 in Q3, but noted that this pool is shrinking.
  • Management continued to highlight portfolio rotation, cost reduction, and defensive positioning as primary strategies, with increasing attention to leveraging new credit facilities and preferred share issuances.
  • Analyst questions in both quarters centered on dividend sustainability, portfolio yield, and potential for share buybacks, reflecting a consistent theme of cautious inquiry.

Risks and Concerns

  • Mehta flagged that "tighter loan spreads continue to weigh on portfolio yields and valuations during the quarter" and referenced persistent supply-demand imbalance in the loan market.
  • Management acknowledged the challenge of growing the fund while it trades below NAV, with Mehta stating the focus remains on "optimizing both the portfolio and the liabilities."
  • The unsold legacy real estate asset remains a portfolio risk, with timing on its sale still uncertain.
  • The percentage of loans rated CCC by S&P was reported at 4.3%, below the 7.5% threshold, and the percentage of loans trading below 80% decreased from 3.1% to 2.6%.

Final Takeaway

Carlyle Credit Income Fund presented a quarter of steady dividend maintenance supported by active portfolio rotation, ongoing refinancing and resets, and a focus on cost reduction. Management highlighted the fund's defensive positioning, confidence in dividend coverage, and readiness to capitalize on future loan market activity. While recurring cash flows and investment income declined quarter-over-quarter, the fund's strategic moves—such as reducing the cost of capital and leveraging both primary and secondary CLO opportunities—aim to sustain yield and support long-term growth, even as tighter loan spreads and market imbalances persist.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Praxis a new buy at BTIG as biotech is a top pick for 2026
data.description:
• BTIG has initiated Praxis Precision Medicines (PRAX) with a buy rating saying that the central nervous system-focused biotech is a top pick for 2026 given it has three assets in the pipeline with blockbuster potential. 
  • The firm has a $424 price target (~130% upside based on Nov. 18 close).
  • Analyst Kambiz Yazdi said the three potential blockbusters are uliacaltamide, vormatrigine, and relutrigine.
  • Uliacaltamide is in phase 3 for essential tremor, vormatrigine is in phase 2/3 for focal onset seizures and generalized epilepsy, and relutrigine is in phase 2/3 for developmental and epileptic encephalopathies.
  • An NDA fling for uliacaltamide is estimated in Q1 2026 while one for relutrigine is expected later in the year.
  • Regarding uliacaltamide, Yazdi wrote, "The unmet need in essential tremors could not be greater, with ~2% of U.S. adults affected and no approved medications since 1967..."

data.title: Amer Sports scores big: Stock climbs to two-month high as Q3 performance offsets fireworks fallout
data.description:
Amer Sports’ (AS) impressive third quarter results and upgraded full year outlook underscores the company’s strong momentum despite a brief disruption in China caused by a fireworks incident.  

Shares continue to gain altitude and are up for a third straight day to a two-month high.

Double-digit growth across product lines and strength in its Chinese and Asia/Pacific markets led Amer Sports (AS) to double its adjusted profits for the third quarter and 240 basis point expansion to its gross profit margin.  

“Our sense is some are still cautious on Amer Sports’ outlook given its high leverage to China and fears around Arc’teryx’s potential slowdown. However, we believe the market underappreciates the strength of Amer’s business model,” UBS analyst Jay Sole said in a note to clients.

Sole points to the total addressable market in athletic wear and sporting equipment, expansion opportunities in direct-to-consumer, specifically for Arc’teryx and Salomon, and deeper international expansion in its Ball and Racquet category.

Citi Research is equally upbeat on Amer Sports’ (AS) growth potential and views it as “one of the best growth stories in retail” given multiple growth drivers across brands, channels, and regions all of which are amplified by margin mix tailwinds.

The company’s exposure to China was a concern during the third quarter after a poorly-planned fireworks display in Tibet angered environmentalists and highlighted the company’s vulnerability to Chinese consumers. The repercussions caused the stock to lose 10% in value over the following four days.

Fortunately, the fallout did little to impact fourth quarter guidance as cooler weather in the region is seen underpinning Outdoor sales, and as tariff headwinds are unlikely to impact demand.

“We remain confident that we are well positioned to manage through a variety of tariff scenarios given our low exposure to the U.S., our pricing power and our clean balance sheet. We continue to expect negligible impact to our group P&L from higher tariffs in 2025 and beyond,” said Andrew Page, CFO of Amer Sports (AS) and CEO of Wilson on the earnings call.

Amer Sports (AS) shares are up 66% year-over-year versus a 12.3% increase in the benchmark S&P index.

data.title: What analysts are saying about Togolf Callaway after its stake sale
data.description:
Topgolf Callaway Brands Corp. (MODG) is being sized up by analysts after the company struck a deal to sell a 60% stake in the Topgolf and Toptracer businesses to Leonard Green & Partners at an implied valuation of approximately $1.1 billion. Upon closing the transaction, the company plans to change its name to Callaway Golf Company and update its ticker symbol to CALY.

Texas Capital analyst Eric Wold said that at a high level, the firm is positive on the proposed transaction as an avenue for the company to both monetize the Topgolf segment for existing shareholders while still retaining an ownership stake and participation in the future growth of Topgolf. "However, we are disappointed by the implied valuation of $1.1B—not only due to the meaningful deterioration from the original ~$2.6 billion valuation when the company originally acquired Topgolf in March 2021, but also because it’s well below the implied $2.2 billion valuation for Topgolf that we included in our sum-of-the-parts analysis," highlighted Wold.

It was noted that the Topgolf business has been challenged recently, with eight straight quarters of negative same-venue sales before the most recent quarter’s pivot back into positive territory; however, the deal valuation does not give full credit for the strong brand equity of the business or the opportunity for renewed growth, according to Wold.

Roth Capital analyst George Kelly said the firm would have preferred an outright sale but is pleased nonetheless and would take advantage of the surprising negative market reaction.

Jefferies analyst Randal Konik said the potential $1.1 billion stake sale of Topgolf is a best-case scenario for the company, enabling a simplification of the business, renewed focus on Callaway’s core golf equipment brand, and removal of valuation and certain overhangs. 

"In our view, the potential sale of Topgolf is a transformative event for MODG, representing a decisive step toward biz simplification, strategic clarity, and value creation," wrote Konik. "By shedding the complexities and risks of the venue business, MODG is poised to unlock its full potential as a pure-play golf equipment leader," he added.

All three firms still have a Buy rating on Topgolf Callaway Brands Corp. (MODG).

Shares of Topgolf Callaway Brands (MODG) were down 7.7% in early afternoon trading.

data.title: Schneider Electric, Digital Realty announce $373M supply capacity agreement
data.description:
• Schneider Electric (OTCPK:SBGSY) (OTCPK:SBGSF) announced on Wednesday that Digital Realty (DLR) announced has signed a $373 million supply capacity agreement for Uninterruptible Power Supply ((UPS)), Low Voltage Switchgear ((LVS)), and Pre-Fabricated Skids. 
  • The announcement was unveiled at Schneider Electric's Innovation Summit North America in Las Vegas.
  • "As demand for digital infrastructure continues to accelerate, securing reliable capacity has become more critical than ever," said Jeff Tapley, Chief Operating Officer, Digital Realty. "This agreement with Schneider Electric strengthens our ability to scale with confidence, while maintaining the flexibility and resilience our customers expect. By deepening our collaboration, we're creating a more agile and future-ready supply chain to support the next phase of growth."
  • DLR is -0.98% to $158.05.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Amentum Holdings gains after bullish Citron post
data.description:
• Amentum Holdings (AMTM) rose 8.3% after a bullish post from Citron's Andrew Left.
  • "Trump just made AMTM the most important company in America that nobody's heard of....$55," Citron's Andrew Left wrote in a post on X. 
  • "Amentum manages 90% of America's nuclear infrastructure for the DOE," Citron's Left added. 
  • Citron sees 100% upside for the shares.

data.title: Pfizer resolves Texas lawsuit over adulterated ADHD drug in $41.5M settlement
data.description:
The Texas Attorney General announced on Wednesday that Pfizer (PFE) and privately owned Tris Pharma have reached a $41.5M settlement to resolve a lawsuit alleging that the duo provided an adulterated attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder medication to the state.

According to the suit filed by Texas AG Ken Paxton in 2023, Pfizer (PFE) and Tris supplied an ADHD medication called Quillivant XR to children on Texas Medicaid despite the drug’s tendency to fail quality control tests due to faulty production methods.

The lawsuit further claimed that the duo continued to manipulate Quillivant's testing methods, breaching federal and state laws to ensure that the liquid medication meets regulatory standards and gets included in the Texas Medicaid program.

“Pfizer and Tris Pharma provided adulterated drugs to children for years and changed test results in order to obtain the benefit of taxpayer-funded Medicaid reimbursement,” Paxton said, adding, “under my watch, Big Pharma will not escape justice for lying about the effectiveness of its drugs.” 

Pfizer (PFE) denied any wrongdoing as part of the settlement, Reuters reported. The New York-based pharma giant added in a statement that its investigations found no impact from Paxton’s claims on Quillivant’s safety when used in children.

data.title: Tyler Technologies acquires CloudGavel
data.description:
• Tyler Technologies (TYL) said on Wednesday it has acquired CloudGavel, a provider of electronic warrant solutions.
  • The company said that the acquisition complements and augments Tyler’s existing solutions, including its courts and public safety solutions, which offers enhanced real-time data access for law enforcement agencies and judicial officers. 
  • The management and staff of CloudGavel will become part of Tyler’s Courts & Justice Division, said the company. 
  • Source: Press Release
